segments.s357-g25: کنون (an adverb) begins the sentence ('now'). من بگویم is first-person present/subjunctive used here as a narrative/future-oriented 'I will say/I will tell'; بگویم is the verb 'to say' and governs the following object. سخن کو بگفت is a relative clause functioning as that object: سخن 'words/speech' + کو, an archaic/poetic relative 'that/which' (not an interrogative here), + بگفت, past tense 'he said' — literally 'the words which he said.'
segments.s357-g26: منم زنده is reported speech: منم 'I am' (emphatic 'I myself am') + زنده 'alive' — the quoted utterance. The narrative then contradicts it: او گشت با خاک جفت is a main-clause comment on his fate: او 'he' + گشت past 'became' (i.e., 'came to be') + با 'with' + خاک 'earth/soil' + جفت 'paired/joined', a poetic idiom meaning 'joined with the earth' (i.e., buried/died).
segments.s357-g9: A nominal phrase made of the title 'شهنشاه' + proper name 'محمود' followed by a participial epithet 'گیرنده شهر' ('seizer/conqueror of the city'). The present-participial noun 'گیرنده' is used as an epithet modifying the name; word order is title → name → epithet + its object.
segments.s357-g10: 'ز شادی' is an adverbial phrase using poetic 'ز' (= از) meaning 'from/with joy'. 'به هر کس' is the indirect object 'to every person'. 'رسانیده' is a perfect/participial verbal form meaning 'has caused to reach/has brought' and governs the object phrase. Final 'بهر' is a poetic preposition/adverb ('for/unto') adding a concessive/beneficial nuance. Overall: 'has caused joy to reach every one.'
segments.s357-g11: 'از امروز' = 'from today' (temporal adverbial). 'تا' is the limit marker 'until', followed by 'سال هشتاد و پنج' = 'the year eighty-five' (note the ‘و’ functioning as 'and' inside the numeral: eighty and five). This whole phrase sets the temporal span.
segments.s357-g12: Two parallel verb phrases with enclitic object '-ش' (his/it). 'بکاهدش' (بـ + کاهد + ش) uses the present/optative prefix بـ to express a wish/result ('may it lessen for him'); its object 'رنج' = 'suffering'. Coordinating 'و' links the second clause 'نکاهدش' (negative with ن- 'do not reduce' plus enclitic) taking 'گنج' = 'treasure' as object. The sense is a blessing/wish: suffering decrease, treasure not decrease.
segments.s357-g13: 'ازین پس' ('از + این' fused here) = 'after this / from now on'. 'به چین' marks the destination 'to China'. 'اندر آرد سپاه' is an archaic order where 'اندر' = 'into/within' precedes the verb 'آرد' ('brings/leads') and 'سپاه' = 'army' is the object; together meaning 'he led/put an army into China.' The verb form 'آرد' is classical narrative present used for past action.
segments.s357-g14: 'همه مهتران' = 'all the chiefs/great men' (subject). 'برگشایند' is a plural verbal form 'they opened/cleared' (classical form used in narrative) and takes 'راه' = 'the road/way' as object: 'they opened/cleared the way.' Word order is subject → verb → object.
segments.s357-g15: 'نبایدش' is the impersonal/modal 'نباید' ('it must not / ought not') with enclitic '-ش' ('to him/it'), giving the sense 'it was not proper for him'. It is followed by the infinitive 'گفتن' ('to say/call'), then the object 'کسی را' (accusative marked by 'را' = 'anyone'), and the adjective 'درشت' functioning as object complement: 'to call someone coarse/harsh.' Word order: modal → infinitive → object → complement.
segments.s357-g16: 'همه تاج شاهانش' = 'all the crowns of his kings' (possessive cluster: crowns of the kings that belong to him). 'آمد' is the past/ narrative verb 'came', and 'به مشت' is a prepositional phrase 'into (his) fist/hand'. Together: 'all the crowns of the kings came into his fist.'
segments.s357-g1: چنان در sentence‑initial position is an adverb meaning 'thus/so' and modifies the verb دید. دید is a past tense verb 'saw/beheld'. گوینده is the agent/narrator ('the one who speaks/tells') and precedes the verb in this poetic word order. یک شب به خواب is a time/place adjunct: یک شب 'one night' + به خواب 'in a dream/while sleeping' (به here marks the state/into which the seeing occurs).
segments.s357-g2: که introduces a subordinate (reported) clause 'that…'. یک جام is the direct object ('a cup'). می‌داشتی is an older/poetic past form of داشتن; here it functions as past imperfect/past-tense 'he was holding / he had' (the -ی ending is common in older Persian poetic forms). چون is a simile marker 'like/as', and گلاب names the comparison 'rose‑water'.
segments.s357-g3: دقیقی is a proper name here (the poet Daqiqi). ز جایی is a prepositional phrase 'from somewhere' (ز = 'from', جایی = 'some place'). پدید آمدی is a poetic past form meaning 'appeared' or 'came forth' (آمدی is used in classical poetry for third‑person past in contexts of meter; functionally 'he appeared').
segments.s357-g4: بران is a compound/poetic preposition meaning 'on/thereupon' or 'concerning that' and links to جام 'the cup' (بران جام = 'on/over that cup' or 'concerning that cup'). می‌داستانها is the verbal noun/verb 'to tell/recount tales' (here plural/collective), and زدی is the past tense of زدن used in the sense 'began/struck/performed'—together giving the idiom 'he began to tell tales' (زدی functions as the action marker in this idiomatic phrase).
segments.s357-g5: به فردوسی is an indirect object phrase 'to/for Ferdowsi' (به = 'to/for'). آواز دادی is a past tense expression 'you gave voice/you called out' (دادی = second‑person past 'you gave'); که here begins the quoted injunction. می in this clause is the noun 'wine' (not the verbal prefix).
segments.s357-g6: مخور is the negative imperative 'do not drink' (second person). جز is the exception marker 'except', بر آیین is a prepositional phrase 'according to the custom/rite' (بر = 'on/according to', آیین = 'custom/ritual'). کاوس کی functions here as the name/title 'Kay Kavus' (the epic king); syntactically the clause forbids drinking wine except in the manner prescribed by Kay Kavus.
segments.s357-g7: که again introduces a clause. شاهی here denotes 'kingship' or 'a royal position' (شاهی ز گیتی گزیدی = 'you chose/assumed kingship from the world'). گزیدی is past 'you chose/selected' and the following که begins a further explanatory clause about بخت ('fortune').
segments.s357-g8: بدو is a short prepositional/clitic form 'to him/unto him'. نازد is a poetic verb here meaning roughly 'is proud of' or 'flirts/cossets' (in context 'Fortune is proud of him' or 'Fortune favors him'); the following و coordinates a list: لشگر 'army', و تاج 'and crown', و تخت 'and throne' — a catalog of regal possessions or honors associated with that favor. The verbs are elided, so the nouns are understood as the things bestowed or present.
segments.s357-g17: بدین نامه‌گر is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to/for this messenger/scribe' built from the prefix ب- + this (here orthography split); چند is an interrogative 'how/why so much'; بشتافتی is a past 2nd-person singular form 'you hastened' (شتافتن → بشتافتن) placed after the adverbial phrase, a typical old/poetic word order.
segments.s357-g18: کنون is a temporal adverb 'now'; هرچ is a free-relative 'whatever/whichever'; جستی is 2nd-person singular past of جستن here = 'you sought'; همه is an emphatic object 'all/everything'; یافتی is 2nd-person singular past 'you found/obtained' — together: 'now, whatever you sought, you have found everything.'
segments.s357-g19: ازین باره = 'about/regarding this matter' (از = 'concerning', ین = orthographic variant of این), من پیش گفتم places the speaker (من) and the temporal adverb پیش 'previously' before گفتم ('I said / I spoke') — a normal classical sequence meaning 'I already spoke on this matter.'
segments.s357-g20: سخن را uses را as the definite/object marker to topicalize 'the speech/words'; نیامد is negative past/impersonal meaning 'did not come / could not (be made to)'; سراسر به بن is an adverbial phrase 'entirely/to the root/foundation' (سراسر 'throughout', به بن 'to the root'), so the clause means 'the words would not come entirely to their foundation' (i.e. I could not set the whole speech down).
segments.s357-g21: ز + proper names (گشتاسپ و ارجاسپ) introduces the topical/partitive object 'about Gushtasp and Arjasp'; بیتی هزار is an older poetic ordering of noun + numeral meaning 'a thousand couplets/verses' (numeral after noun is common in classical Persian poetry).
segments.s357-g22: بگفتم is the past 1st-person 'I said / I recited'; سرآمد here means 'the end/conclusion' and is used predicatively; مرا (split in the segmentation) is the dative/possessive 'to/for me' and را topicalizes the phrase, so روزگار سرآمد مرا (elliptically ordered) means 'my time/fate came to an end.' Poetic word order allows the predicate and pronominal elements to appear without an explicit auxiliary.
segments.s357-g23: گر is the conditional 'if'; آن مایه 'that matter/thing' is the conditional antecedent; نزد شهنشه is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of/before the shah'; رسد is 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive 'reaches / comes to' — classical Persian uses this form for potential/result clauses introduced by گر.
segments.s357-g24: روان من 'my soul' is the subject; از خاک بر مه is an ablative–allative phrase 'from the earth toward the moon' (از = 'from', بر = 'to/toward', مه = 'moon'); رسد (again 3rd-person sg.) states the resulting motion 'will reach/ascend' — the whole is the protasis/apodosis pair with the previous conditional.

sentences.s357-s13: Now I will tell the words that he spoke: "I am alive" — yet he became one with the earth.
sentences.s357-s5: The Emperor Mahmud, conqueror of the city, has brought joy to every person.
sentences.s357-s6: From today until the year eighty-five, may his suffering be lessened and may his treasure not be diminished.
sentences.s357-s7: After this, he led an army into China; all the chiefs cleared the way.
sentences.s357-s8: He ought not to call anyone harshly; all the crowns of the kings came into his fist.
sentences.s357-s1: Thus the narrator one night saw in a dream that he was holding a cup like rose‑water.
sentences.s357-s2: Daqiqi appeared from somewhere, and on that cup he began to tell tales.
sentences.s357-s3: You cried out to Ferdowsi, 'Do not drink wine except according to the custom of Kay Kavus.'
sentences.s357-s4: That you took kingship from the world — that Fortune is proud of him, and (gives him) army, crown, and throne.
sentences.s357-s9: Why did you hasten so much to this messenger; now whatever you sought you have found it all.
sentences.s357-s10: About this matter I spoke before, but the speech did not come wholly to its foundation.
sentences.s357-s11: I recited a thousand couplets about Gushtasp and Arjasp; my days (my fate) came to an end.
sentences.s357-s12: If that matter reaches the shah, my soul will rise from the dust to the moon.
